frozen_components: SAE encoder/decoder (JumpReLU SAE, Gemma Scope layer 12)
architecture.summary: SAE-Targeted Steering (SAE-TS) is a steering method that operates by first learning a linear effect approximator mapping steering vectors to their effects on SAE feature activations. The system measures steering effects by (1) generating text rollouts from a steered model (steering vector added to residual stream at layer 12), (2) passing rollouts back through the model to extract SAE feature activations, and (3) computing mean feature activation differences between steered and unsteered outputs. A linear approximator is trained on 50,000 steering vectors and their measured effects. To construct a targeted steering vector for a desired feature, the method extracts the target feature's decoder column from the learned effect matrix, subtracts a bias term, and normalizes. An adaptive scaling factor is selected per vector to raise cross-entropy loss by 0.5 above baseline.
trained_components: linear effect approximator (50k steering vector samples, Adam optimization, MSE loss)

biblio.title: Improving Steering Vectors by Targeting Sparse Autoencoder Features

claims.statement: SAE-TS balances steering effects with coherence better than CAA and SAE feature steering, when evaluated on a range of tasks

claims.dataset: 9 steering tasks (Anger, Christian, Conspiracy, French, London, Love, Praise, Want to die, Wedding) on Gemma-2-2B
claims.statement: SAE-TS outperforms baseline methods on 7 of 9 steering tasks when measured at peak Behavioral*Coherence score
On the London task, SAE-TS achieves Behavioral*Coherence score of 0.5380 at peak, significantly outperforming CAA (0.0476) and SAE steering (0.0061)

At high scaling factors well above optimal scale on the London task, SAE-TS exhibits an interesting failure mode where output diversity collapses and the model produces text focused entirely on fashion shows and art exhibitions in London
All investigations were performed using Gemma-2 2B and 9B, both sharing the same distilled model architecture, so results may not transfer to other models

SAE-TS can only steer towards features present in the SAE used to measure feature effects; some concepts do not appear as individual SAE features (feature splitting), and niche features may only appear in extremely large SAEs

On Gemma-2-9B, SAE-TS achieves average Behavioral*Coherence score of 0.3376 across 9 tasks, higher than CAA (0.2531) and SAE steering (0.2184); however CAA beats SAE-TS on 4 of 9 tasks and ties on 1 task

The feature effects measurement method uses SAEs to understand the causal effect of steering vector interventions by measuring changes in SAE feature activations between steered and unsteered model outputs
